questions.question: What does the 'su' command stand for in the UNIX system?
options: System User
Super User
Security Utility
System Update
questions.explanation: The 'su' command stands for 'Substitute User'. It allows a user to switch to another user's account.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the '-' option in the 'su' command?
options: It allows the user to switch to the root user account.
It allows the user to switch to the current user's account.
It allows the user to execute a command with super user privileges.
It allows the user to switch to the root user's home directory.
questions.explanation: The '-' option in the 'su' command allows the user to execute a command with super user privileges.

questions.question: How can you switch to the root user account using the 'su' command?
options: su -
su root
su root$
su root!
questions.explanation: The correct way to switch to the root user account using the 'su' command is 'su -'.

questions.question: What happens when you type 'su' without any options in the command line?
options: You will be prompted to enter the password of the root user.
You will be prompted to enter the password of the current user.
You will be switched to the current user's account.
You will be switched to the root user's account.
questions.explanation: When you type 'su' without any options in the command line, you will be prompted to enter the password of the root user.

questions.question: Can you switch to the root user account using the 'su' command with just the root password?
options: Yes, use 'su' with just the root password.
Yes, use 'su -' with the current user's password.
No, you need to use the '-' option.
No, you need to use the 'su' command multiple times.
questions.explanation: Yes, you can switch to the root user account using the 'su' command with just the root password.
